questions.text: Who drafted the Indian Constitution?
questions.explanation: Dr. B.R. Ambedkar chaired the Drafting Committee of the Indian Constitution.

questions.text: The Magna Carta was signed in 1215 and limited the powers of the English King.
questions.explanation: The Magna Carta was indeed signed in 1215 by King John.

questions.text: Which article of the Indian Constitution grants Fundamental Rights?
questions.explanation: Articles 12–35 of the Indian Constitution deal with Fundamental Rights.

questions.text: Match the independence leaders with their independence movements:
questions.explanation: These leaders are associated with their respective freedom movements.

questions.text: The Sepoy Mutiny occurred in which year?
questions.explanation: The Revolt of 1857 began in May 1857.

questions.text: The French Revolution took place in the 18th century.
questions.explanation: The French Revolution began in 1789.

questions.text: Which of these were Mughal emperors? (Select all correct)
questions.explanation: Akbar, Shah Jahan, and Aurangzeb were Mughal emperors.

questions.text: Assertion: Democracy requires a constitution. Reason: A constitution defines the structure and powers of government.
questions.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

questions.text: The Universal Declaration of Human Rights was adopted in which year?
questions.explanation: The UDHR was adopted by the UN General Assembly on 10 December 1948.

questions.text: The Indian Constitution currently has ______ Schedules.
questions.explanation: The Constitution of India currently contains 12 Schedules.

questions.text: In which year did the First World War begin?
questions.explanation: The First World War began in 1914 following the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand.

questions.text: The Industrial Revolution first began in Great Britain.
questions.explanation: The Industrial Revolution originated in Great Britain in the late 18th century before spreading globally.

questions.text: The Quit India Movement was launched in the year ________.
questions.explanation: Mahatma Gandhi launched the Quit India Movement on August 8, 1942, demanding an end to British rule.

questions.text: Which of the following nations were part of the Axis Powers during World War II? (Select all correct)
questions.explanation: Germany, Italy, and Japan formed the core of the Axis Powers during World War II.

questions.text: Assertion: The Cold War did not involve direct large-scale military combat between the USA and the USSR. Reason: Both superpowers possessed nuclear weapons, leading to the doctrine of Mutually Assured Destruction.
questions.explanation: Both the assertion and the reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why direct military conflict was avoided.

questions.text: Match the prominent Indian historical figures with their popular titles:
questions.explanation: These leaders were affectionately given these titles by the public and their contemporaries for their roles in India's independence.

questions.text: Who was the first President of the United States of America?
questions.explanation: George Washington served as the first President of the United States from 1789 to 1797.

questions.text: The United Nations was formed immediately after World War I in 1919.
questions.explanation: The League of Nations was formed in 1919. The United Nations was formed in 1945 after World War II.

questions.text: Article 14 of the Indian Constitution guarantees the Right to ________.
questions.explanation: Article 14 states that the State shall not deny to any person equality before the law or the equal protection of the laws.

questions.text: The Russian Revolution, which led to the rise of the Soviet Union, occurred in which year?
questions.explanation: The Russian Revolution took place in 1917, overthrowing the Tsarist autocracy.

questions.text: Which of the following are considered ancient Indian texts? (Select all correct)
questions.explanation: The Vedas, Upanishads, and Mahabharata are foundational ancient Indian texts.

questions.text: Assertion: India is called a Republic. Reason: The head of the Indian state is an elected President rather than a hereditary monarch.
questions.explanation: Both statements are true. A republic is a form of government where the country is considered a public matter and the head of state is elected.

questions.text: Match the ancient civilizations with the river valleys where they developed:
questions.explanation: Early human civilizations primarily developed along major river systems which provided water and fertile soil.

questions.text: Who is credited with discovering the direct sea route from Europe to India in 1498?
questions.explanation: Vasco da Gama reached Calicut in 1498, establishing the first direct sea route from Europe to India.

questions.text: The Constitution of India is the longest written national constitution in the world.
questions.explanation: With a preamble, hundreds of articles, and 12 schedules, the Indian Constitution is the longest written national constitution.

questions.text: The First Battle of Panipat was fought in the year ________.
questions.explanation: The First Battle of Panipat took place in 1526 between Babur and Ibrahim Lodi, marking the beginning of the Mughal Empire in India.

questions.text: Which of the following are Fundamental Rights guaranteed by the Indian Constitution? (Select all correct)
options: B. Right to Freedom of Religion
questions.explanation: Right to Property was removed as a Fundamental Right by the 44th Amendment, and Right to Strike is a legal right, not a fundamental one.

questions.text: Assertion: The Jallianwala Bagh massacre of 1919 caused widespread outrage across India. Reason: General Dyer ordered his troops to fire on an unarmed crowd of men, women, and children.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct, and the brutal nature of the massacre directly caused the national outrage.

questions.text: Match the Indian dynasties with their respective founders:
questions.explanation: These are the historical founders of three of the most significant empires in Indian history.

questions.text: Who gave the famous slogan 'Give me blood, and I shall give you freedom'?
questions.explanation: Subhas Chandra Bose delivered this famous quote to motivate the Indian National Army.

questions.text: Apartheid was an institutionalized system of racial segregation in South Africa.
questions.explanation: Apartheid was a policy that governed relations between South Africa's white minority and nonwhite majority from 1948 until the 1990s.

questions.text: The capital city of the ancient Mauryan Empire was ________.
questions.explanation: Pataliputra, near modern-day Patna, served as the capital of the Mauryan Empire.

questions.text: Who among the following were prominent founding leaders of the Non-Aligned Movement? (Select all correct)
questions.explanation: Nehru (India), Tito (Yugoslavia), and Nasser (Egypt) were key architects of the Non-Aligned Movement.

questions.text: Assertion: The League of Nations ultimately failed to prevent the outbreak of World War II. Reason: The League lacked an armed force of its own and several major powers, including the US, were not members.
questions.explanation: Both statements are true. The structural weaknesses of the League of Nations directly contributed to its failure.

questions.text: Match the historical documents with the years they were adopted:
pairs: French Declaration of the Rights of Man
questions.explanation: These are key dates for some of the most important political documents in modern history.

questions.text: Who served as the first Governor-General of independent India?
questions.explanation: Lord Mountbatten was the last Viceroy and the first Governor-General of independent India.

questions.text: The Berlin Wall, which divided East and West Berlin, fell in the year 1989.
questions.explanation: The fall of the Berlin Wall in November 1989 was a pivotal event marking the end of the Cold War.

questions.text: The cultural movement known as the Renaissance began in the country of ________.
questions.explanation: The Renaissance originated in Italian city-states like Florence in the 14th century before spreading across Europe.

questions.text: Which of the following are the three primary branches of the Indian Government? (Select all correct)
questions.explanation: The Indian Government is divided into the Executive, Legislative, and Judicial branches to ensure a separation of powers.

questions.text: Assertion: The Right to Information (RTI) Act promotes transparency and accountability in India. Reason: It empowers citizens to seek information from public authorities.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct, and empowering citizens to access information is the exact reason it promotes transparency.

questions.text: Match the significant Indian battles with their corresponding years:
questions.explanation: These battles were turning points in Indian history, establishing new power dynamics in the subcontinent.

questions.text: The political ideology of Fascism was founded by which 20th-century leader?
questions.explanation: Benito Mussolini founded the National Fascist Party in Italy after World War I.

questions.text: The Indus Valley Civilization was known for its advanced urban planning and drainage systems.
questions.explanation: Cities like Harappa and Mohenjo-Daro featured sophisticated grid layouts, baked brick houses, and extensive drainage.

questions.text: The first Indian woman to become President of the UN General Assembly was Vijaya Lakshmi ________.
questions.explanation: Vijaya Lakshmi Pandit was elected as the President of the eighth session of the UN General Assembly in 1953.

questions.text: Which of the following were major causes of the French Revolution? (Select all correct)
options: A. Social inequality and the estate system
B. Severe economic crisis and debt
C. The invention of the printing press
E. The discovery of the Americas
questions.explanation: Social disparities, a bankrupt treasury, and new philosophical ideas heavily contributed to the outbreak of the French Revolution.

questions.text: Assertion: Emperor Ashoka eventually abandoned military conquest and embraced Buddhism. Reason: He was deeply moved and horrified by the massive loss of life and destruction during the Kalinga War.
questions.explanation: Both the assertion and the reason are true, and the bloodshed at Kalinga was the direct catalyst for his transformation.

questions.text: Match the early Presidents of India with their chronological order of service:
questions.explanation: These leaders served sequentially as the first three Presidents of independent India.

questions.text: Which amendment to the Indian Constitution is often referred to as the 'Mini Constitution' due to its comprehensive changes?
questions.explanation: Enacted during the Emergency in 1976, the 42nd Amendment made widespread changes to the Constitution.

questions.text: The partition of Bengal, ordered by Lord Curzon, took place in 1905.
questions.explanation: The partition of Bengal was enacted in 1905, dividing the largely Muslim eastern areas from the largely Hindu western areas.

questions.text: The ancient center of higher learning, ________ University, was located in present-day Bihar.
questions.explanation: Nalanda was a renowned Mahavihara (Buddhist monastery) and one of the greatest centers of learning in the ancient world.
